specialized.task_coverage: Fry station only: baskets, timing, and repetitive fryer handling inside quick-service kitchens.
specialized.claim_supported: Commercial kitchen deployments exist, but the claim is limited to a narrow fry-station slice rather than the full fast-food role.
specialized.note: Flippy is relevant because it attacks a real, injury-prone fast-food subtask. The evidence should not be read as broad counter-worker automation: the system covers frying workflow, while ordering, assembly, stocking, cleaning, and customer exceptions remain outside scope.

sources.label: Restaurant Dive, Jan 2025
sources.supports: Describes new Flippy version and early 2025 installations, with deployment count caveat.

sources.label: Miso Robotics, Jan 2025
sources.supports: Describes the next-generation fry-station system and planned installations.

humanoid.task_coverage: Adjacent food-handling and kitchen manipulation demos; no paid counter-service deployment.
humanoid.claim_supported: Robot foundation-model demos show relevant manipulation primitives, not occupation-targeting deployment.
humanoid.note: The strongest humanoid-adjacent evidence is general manipulation in kitchens and homes. It is useful for the threshold-clearance argument, but it remains lab evidence rather than a commercial fast-food system.

sources.label: Physical Intelligence, Apr 2025
sources.supports: Shows open-world manipulation generalization, including kitchen-style tasks.

occupations.title: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand

specialized.task_coverage: Trailer unloading: box lifting, transfer, and induction from trailers to conveyors.
specialized.claim_supported: Reported order activity and pilots support a narrow commercial unloading task, not the full material-mover occupation.
specialized.note: Trailer unloading is one of the clearest manual material-handling slices because the task is repetitive, physically demanding, and measurable. The remaining occupation includes many other warehouse, stock, and freight-handling tasks.

sources.label: Sourcing Journal, Dec 2025
sources.supports: Reports UPS order details and prior logistics pilots.

humanoid.task_coverage: Tote movement and handoff in live fulfillment settings.
humanoid.claim_supported: Commercial-site tote movement supports paid or customer-facing pilot evidence for logistics, but not broad material-mover substitution.
humanoid.note: Digit provides the strongest public humanoid evidence in the top-ten list because it has moved high volumes of totes in a live logistics operation. The task remains bounded and co-located with human workflows.

sources.label: Agility Robotics, Nov 2025
sources.supports: Reports more than 100,000 totes moved at GXO's Flowery Branch facility.

specialized.task_coverage: Distribution-center storage, retrieval, sequencing, and case handling.
specialized.claim_supported: Multi-site warehouse automation is well supported; occupation-level stocker/order-filler substitution is not directly estimated.
specialized.note: Symbotic is strong evidence that stock-flow tasks can move from individual workers into engineered warehouse systems. The better interpretation is task reallocation inside distribution networks rather than a complete occupation replacement measure.

sources.label: Walmart and Symbotic, May 2022
sources.supports: Announces expansion of Symbotic systems to all 42 Walmart regional distribution centers over coming years.

sources.label: Symbotic, Jan 2025
sources.supports: Describes APD agreement, 400-system opportunity, and possible $5B-plus backlog increase.

humanoid.task_coverage: Industrial and logistics partner pilots in controlled zones.
humanoid.claim_supported: Public partnerships and funding support serious pilot activity, but public task-performance evidence is thinner than for fixed warehouse automation.
humanoid.note: Apollo is relevant because its customer set overlaps logistics and manufacturing. The evidence is still mostly partnership and financing signal, so it should sit below observed high-volume task operation.

sources.label: Apptronik, Feb 2026
sources.supports: Reports funding and partnerships with Mercedes-Benz, GXO Logistics, and Jabil.

sources.label: TechCrunch, Feb 2026
sources.supports: Reports valuation and strategic partnerships.

specialized.task_coverage: Food running, bussing support, and tray transport in restaurants and hospitality settings.
specialized.claim_supported: Multi-site service-robot deployment is credible, but it covers transport tasks rather than the whole waiter role.
specialized.note: Servi is a useful case because it shows that a visible slice of table-service labor can be mechanized without solving the social and judgment-heavy parts of waiting tables.

sources.label: TechCrunch, Jan 2025
sources.supports: Reports LG's majority stake and Bear Robotics deployments across hospitality settings.

humanoid.task_coverage: No credible humanoid task coverage identified
humanoid.claim_supported: Absence of a credible public humanoid deployment targeting this occupation as of April 2026.
humanoid.note: No credible public humanoid deployment appears to target table-service work directly. The form factor is less relevant than smaller mobile service robots for the transport slice.

occupations.title: Janitors and Cleaners, Except Maids and Housekeeping Cleaners

specialized.task_coverage: Autonomous floor scrubbing in airports, malls, warehouses, and large facilities.
specialized.claim_supported: Multi-site autonomous cleaning is credible for floor care; restrooms, trash, detail cleaning, stairs, and clutter remain outside the covered slice.
specialized.note: Janitorial automation is already real for predictable floor coverage. It is also a clean example of why task-level coverage matters: floor scrubbing can be advanced while the occupation remains far from fully automated.

sources.label: The Robot Report, Feb 2024
sources.supports: Describes Neo 2 updates and deployed cleaning use cases.

humanoid.task_coverage: Adjacent home tidying, surface wiping, and laundry claims; no commercial janitorial deployment.
humanoid.claim_supported: Product claims and early-access positioning are relevant to home chores, not facility janitorial substitution.
humanoid.note: NEO belongs here only as adjacent evidence about general manipulation in domestic spaces. It should be read as a frontier signal, not a janitorial labor-market deployment.

sources.label: 1X Technologies, NEO product page
sources.supports: Describes NEO home chores, Expert Mode, and remote-control features.

specialized.task_coverage: Long-haul hub-to-hub Class 8 freight lanes, not local delivery, loading, inspection, or customer interaction.
specialized.claim_supported: Commercial driverless freight and 2026 route expansion are supported; occupation-level driver substitution remains lane- and operating-domain-specific.
specialized.note: Aurora is one of the strongest frontier cases because it has moved paid freight without a driver on public roads. The bounded operational domain matters: heavy-truck driving includes many routes and non-driving tasks outside the current lane network.

sources.label: TechCrunch, May 2025
sources.supports: Reports commercial driverless service launch between Dallas and Houston.

sources.label: TechCrunch, Feb 2026
sources.supports: Reports expanded routes and 250,000 driverless miles as of January 2026.

humanoid.task_coverage: No credible humanoid task coverage identified
humanoid.claim_supported: Absence of a credible public humanoid deployment targeting this occupation as of April 2026.
humanoid.note: A humanoid is not the relevant system for long-haul truck driving. The frontier is autonomous vehicle software and trucking operations, not human-form robots.

specialized.task_coverage: Inspection rounds, asset monitoring, thermal sensing, and data capture in industrial facilities.
specialized.claim_supported: Robotic inspection deployment is well supported; hands-on repair, diagnosis, and signoff remain outside scope.
specialized.note: Spot is strong evidence for inspection augmentation, not for autonomous general maintenance. It changes what workers can monitor and how often, while repair remains human-centered.

sources.label: Boston Dynamics, 2025
sources.supports: Describes Spot deployments and industrial inspection use cases.

humanoid.task_coverage: Adjacent whole-body manipulation and factory parts handling demos; no customer maintenance deployment.
humanoid.claim_supported: Atlas demonstrates relevant mobility and manipulation primitives, not occupation-targeting deployment.
humanoid.note: Atlas is important as a capability marker for ladders, stairs, parts, and tools, but the public evidence is still demonstration-stage for this occupation.

sources.label: Boston Dynamics, Atlas electric
sources.supports: Introduces electric Atlas and its intended industrial learning path.

specialized.task_coverage: Machine tending, light assembly, pick-place, packaging, and repetitive industrial assistance.
specialized.claim_supported: Broad cobot deployment supports augmentation across assembly tasks, but not full automation of miscellaneous assembly occupations.
specialized.note: Cobots are the cleanest example of an augmentation pathway: they can shift repetitive handling while workers retain setup, quality judgment, fixture changes, and exception handling.

sources.label: Robotics 24/7, May 2025
sources.supports: Reports UR15 launch and worldwide UR cobot deployment figures.

humanoid.task_coverage: Automotive sheet-metal loading and adjacent factory material handling.
humanoid.claim_supported: BMW pilot evidence supports a bounded manufacturing task; 2026 production claims show fleet scaling, not deployment impact.
humanoid.note: Figure is compelling because the BMW task is a real production-adjacent assembly workflow. The right claim is still narrow: sheet-metal loading, not the full assembler/fabricator occupation.

sources.label: Figure, Nov 2025
sources.supports: Reports 11-month BMW Spartanburg deployment, 90,000-plus parts loaded, and 30,000-plus X3 vehicles.

sources.label: Figure, Apr 2026
sources.supports: Reports Figure 03 production ramp and BotQ manufacturing progress.

specialized.task_coverage: Automated salad/bowl makeline in a constrained menu and store format.
specialized.claim_supported: Restaurant makeline deployments support a narrow food-assembly task, not restaurant cooking generally.
specialized.note: Infinite Kitchen shows that menu engineering can turn parts of restaurant cooking into an automation-friendly flow. That is not the same as a general cook robot across cuisines, layouts, and peak-time failures.

sources.label: Restaurant Technology News, Nov 2025
sources.supports: Reports Infinite Kitchen footprint, expansion plans, and Wonder transaction.

humanoid.task_coverage: Adjacent meal-prep, table-bussing, grocery, and kitchen manipulation demos.
humanoid.claim_supported: Robot foundation-model demos show relevant manipulation capabilities, not restaurant cook deployment.
humanoid.note: Physical Intelligence is valuable as capability evidence for deformable and kitchen objects. It remains a lab/research signal rather than a commercial cooking system.

sources.label: Physical Intelligence, Oct 2024
sources.supports: Reports pi0 generalist policy and kitchen/food-adjacent manipulation tasks.

sources.label: Physical Intelligence, Apr 2025
sources.supports: Reports pi0.5 open-world generalization in home and kitchen-like environments.

specialized.task_coverage: Hospital logistics: supplies, samples, delivery runs, and elevator use. No patient handling or nursing care.
specialized.claim_supported: Hospital logistics automation is supported; nursing-assistant task automation is deliberately not claimed.
specialized.note: Moxi is useful because it shows a real healthcare augmentation pathway while also marking the boundary clearly: logistics can move to robots before patient-facing care does.

sources.label: The Robot Report, Feb 2025
sources.supports: Reports Moxi hospital activity and one-million-pick milestone.

sources.label: TechCrunch, Jan 2026
sources.supports: Reports Serve Robotics acquisition of Diligent Robotics and healthcare logistics focus.

humanoid.task_coverage: Adjacent eldercare and home-assistance positioning; no clinical patient-handling pilot.
humanoid.claim_supported: Product positioning is relevant to home assistance but does not support nursing-assistant deployment.
humanoid.note: NEO should be treated as adjacent home-care evidence. It is not evidence that humanoids can safely perform patient transfers, bathing, toileting, or clinical observation in care settings.

sources.label: 1X Technologies, NEO product page
sources.supports: Describes NEO home assistance, Expert Mode, and remote operation.
